text:
+Jason94
You may have preferred the N64/Gamecube era, but think of all the gamers who didn't have a single 2D Mario (the classic, true Mario game) for 16 years!!

And sales prove that these gamers are the majority. The Wii sold above 100 million, a record that is 5 times more than the Gamecube, and New Super Mario Bros on DS and Wii sold more than 30 million, which puts them among the best-selling games of all time!

Meanwhile, 3D Mario sell very little in comparison... and they take more money to make! and there are much more of them!
The reason why the Wii was made in the first time as a radical change (at first) from Nintendo's direction is because the Gamecube was a failure (financially and in brand). Nintendo was in danger.

So the actual situation is that you are in a relative niche that has been the main focus of Nintendo for 30 years, since Nintendo went full 3D on their consoles.
Look, there have still been more 3D Mario games on the last consoles, with more budget. Wii U and 3DS were essentially new Gamecubes. The Switch trailer shows yet another brand new 3D Mario game...

It's actually more worrying that Nintendo still seems to not understand that abandoning their classic games (3D Mario in favor of 2D Mario, Other M instead of 2D Metroid or Prime, modern Zelda is nothing like classic Zelda, etc.) will only spiral them further down and dissatisfy most of their customers.

3DS and Wii U were failures like the Gamecube. The Switch seems a remedy to this just like the Wii, but they go all the way with a good software direction, unlike what they did in the later years of the Wii (WTF at Other M? Skyward Sword? Galaxy 2? Wii Music? No good motion controls aside from Wii Sports, even after Motion Plus?).
author: @rarumanH

text:
Roruman 2D Super Mario is certainly fun when done right (i.e. in a way that doesn't seem particularly rehashed) - I wouldn't want those games to disappear. They did release a New Super Mario Bros. game and Super Mario Maker for the Wii U, so I don't think they've abandoned the 2D games yet.

Also, could the 3DS really be considered a failure, or at least on the same level as the GameCube and Wii U? Out of the handheld gaming consoles, it's doing the best (although smart devices are side-tracking the handheld gaming consoles).
author: @k-leb4671

text:
Caleb Jaaniste
"2D Super Mario is certainly fun when done right"
I posted the same thing on the Switch trailer and 10 people answered in disagreement. It is weird.

"so I don't think they've abandoned the 2D games yet."
They only did the NSMB U and 2 because the previous games sold 30 million... It was inevitable.
However, these sequels didn't sell 30 million, but something like 6 million, which is good, and still put them as one of their console's best-seller, and definitely generated more revenue than 3D Mario due to its much smaller budget, but it is a risk that Nintendo will soon not feel obligated to continue making it if goes this way. Of course, this is to blame on the game's quality (lazy rehash) and Wii U and 3DS being commercial failures, and not on 2D Mario's demand in general which is very high.
As for Mario Maker, it is dangerous because it gives Nintendo an excuse to not make more 2D Mario. "Now you can make it and play levels from everyone, you have an infinity of Mario sequels!", basically. I really heard people say this to me.

"could the 3DS really be considered a failure"
Yes, it sold less than DS, PSP, Gameboy and Gameboy Advance. It won against the Vita but it's a poor performance for a Nintendo handheld.
Even worse, it was sold at a loss by receiving a price cut very early because it wasn't selling otherwise.
Since the 3DS is just a new kind of Virtual Boy (now that's a failure!), they did the 2DS.

"smart devices are side-tracking the handheld gaming consoles"
This is a myth you hear often but is completely false.
People were saying this too in the 80's and declared that the NES would be a fail because since the Atari crash, only PC Gaming was the future! Why buy a NES when you could buy a Commodore 64 that could play games in 16 bit AND do other things? but history proved this completely wrong.
Consoles and PCs are in different markets. PC gaming never took away marketshare from console gaming.
Portable PCs ("smart" devices) don't from portable consoles (handhelds) either.
Look, smartphones already existed during the original DS's generation, but they didn't take away from it at all.

text:
***** 
Exactly what I'm thinking. New Super Mario Bros. as a whole is starting to contaminate other Mario series and spinoffs. Modern-day Mario Party, for example? Generic NSMB art style and boring gameplay.

And don't even get me started on Paper Mario. Sticker Star and Color Splash are bland and generic because Miyamoto doesn't want the team to come up with new ideas and characters anymore. He just wants to use locations and characters from the NSMB series.

Even the main-series 3D games like 3D Land and World are suffering from this. Rather than being an open adventure where Mario is limited by health rather than power-ups, and where the goal is to collect as many Stars/Shines to unlock more levels, now the goal is simply the exact same as the 2D games, but in 3D; get to the flagpole and never look back. (Unless you want to 100% all collectable items)

It's just saddening to see that those who prefer a different kind of Mario game more like the older 3D ones have nowhere to turn. The games will always be good, but now they're all the same. Let's just hope that Miyamoto was telling the truth when he told IGN that "You might see an all-new kind of Mario in a year or two".

By the way, nice to see you around here.

text:
*****
"I wish they would put more empathis onto Mario's character"
This is one of the worst things you could ask for. We don't play Mario for the story or the characters.
This is a video game, not a novel, comics or movie. Especially Mario... the only place for story in Mario is in Mario RPG.

"I fear he will become that stale submissive robot-escue character with no personality"
Mario has always been Mario for decades. He didn't have a "complex character" but he is the most famous video game character of all time.
He is just an avatar for the player. He has his iconic appearance and he can jump. He is a valiant plumber saving the surreal lands. There you go, that's enough.

"Sunshine to date is probably my favorite preformance of the character's voice even going as far as to add exhausted grunts and whatnot"
Okay, but why is that more important than gameplay and adventure? xd

"But if anything I'd rather have 2D Mario that plays like Mario 64 if they ever are to go back to 2D."
This is already done. Since NSMB, you have to collect several Yoshi coins throughout the levels, like the stars in M64.

"3D Land did the reverse idea of it, so why not?"
because 3D Mario games are expensive and don't sell enough. There needs to be a REAL 2D Mario above all. It is most important. Mario is Nintendo. Mario is 2D Mario. 3D Mario, however neat, is just a spin-off.

"they were just desperate to grab some gold"
Yes, they were very lazy games. It shows how Miyamoto doesn't care about 2D Mario despite it being essential for Nintendo, which makes him a selfish manager.

"NSMB2 is so unimaginative"
Man, you think this is bad? You know what they planned to do for it initially? They were just gonna call it "New Super Mario Bros MII" with the main new feature being to be able to play as a Mii and everything else apparently the same. It would have been the worst Mario game ever.

It is very annoying that despite NSMB's success, Nintendo keeps trying to put it away. Is it not enough that it sold 30 million? Why don't they do a proper sequel with the necessary budget?
It is one of the biggest WTF ever of gaming.
author: @rarumanH

text:
*****
"It needs to be put away because people are sick of it."
This is completely false.
People buy more 2D Mario than 3D Mario. 2D Mario is a best-seller on any system it is. NSMB DS and Wii are among the best-selling games of all time with 30 million units sold each.
How many times must I repeat this fact, backed up by sales of the games? I will just write this as an answer because I want to emphasize it very strongly.

"it doesn't need to be done every single freakin' time"
This is a lie. It hadn't been done for 20 years. For 20 years, there have been 3D Mario and no 2D Mario. Then NSMB. It sells more than 3D Mario. but still, 3D Mario is the only one to get a budget!
What is the truth is that 3D Mario has been done for far too long, every single time since it first existed, and worse at the expense of 2D Mario.
author: @rarumanH

text:
Close, but you can go deeper. Mario jumped the shark with Mario 64. As a disclaimer, it was a good game. That isn't the issue.

Keep in mind that on the NES, there were 4 (!!!) Super Mario Bros games. Each one was a vast improvement upon the previous one (except "Lost Levels"), was very well received, of course selling excellently. There were plenty of Super Mario Land with their own universe (still 2D platformers) handheld games on the Gameboy. It created a "Mario Mania".

On the SNES, a new sequel, Super Mario World (where Yoshi first appeared), was a launch title. People bought the SNES despite its higher price just for this game! but after, there was no more 2D Mario! People were skeptic, and SNES sales were diminishing. Nintendo was first losing to Sega's Genesis with their own 2D platformer, Sonic.
It was only when Nintendo released a new 2D platformer, Donkey Kong Country, that the SNES started to sell well again and eventually drove out the Genesis.

When the N64 came out, it had Mario 64 on it. On this system, every game had to be 3D. Zelda also became 3D there. Much less people bought it. They wondered why there was still no 2D Mario, and just thought that now everything had to be in 3D and that gaming was no longer for them.
Times passes. There is the Gamecube...

Then the DS and the Wii come along. New Super Mario Bros came on DS in 2006, a long return after 16 years and sold incredibly well. It was a real revival. but people were still thinking 2D platformers could only work on handhelds. Surely, there can only be 3D games on the home console, right? Then 3 years later, NSMB Wii came out and sold amazingly. It was a glorious return. Classic Mario was back! 2D platformers were actually not obsolete!
Many lapsed gamers who became disinterested in gaming after it became full-3D only bought it, as well as droves of new gamers, of course. Today, both of those games sold 30 million units, a best-selling record of all time.
Super Mario Galaxy, despite coming 2 years earlier on the Wii and having much more budget (including music performed by an orchestra, lush graphics, and a lot of stuff put into it), sold much, much less, and so did its sequel.

Then there were the 3DS and Wii U which were commercial failures. On those, there was also an even number of 3D Mario and 2D Mario.
However, the 2D Mario were not interesting. The problem is that they were lazy rehashes of their predecessors. There is close to no new content, many annoying new bloat like "collect coins all the time!" that is more ruining the game than anything.
NSMB U and NSMB2 still sold as much as 3D Mario and are at the top of their console sales, despite getting much less budget, as usual, but the number is very far from what the previous ones did.
People bought NSMB because it was the first 2D Mario in a long time, and despite being not as good as Super Mario Bros 3 and World, they added a very good multiplayer experience.
NSMB U was originally planned so badly that it was first called "NSMB Mii"! It was exactly like it sounds... and the final game was rushed for launch and made by a C-team, while Miyamoto was working on his precious time-consuming 3D Mario...
The reason why recent 3D Mario games attempt to mimic 2D games is because Miyamoto is aware that 2D Mario is more popular and desperately wish to convert 2D Mario fans (everyone) to 3D Mario... but this ironically even annoys 3D Mario players now. It is a bad approach and should be dropped. You tried everything, experimented and did what you wanted a lot, but now is the time to save Mario, Nintendo and Gaming. I will explain how to attain the "best of both worlds" in the next lines...

So, now, Nintendo must do worthy sequels. Really put the focus on classic, true, real, mainline, 2D Mario and add new things into it, with care and effort.

- Galaxy had an orchestra! NSMB has mediocre music (single gimmicky instrument + "wah wah"...). So it should be obvious what to do.
- 3D Mario in general has better production values, like lush last-gen graphics, while NSMB is stale. 2D Mario deserves the same glorious treatment!! You can still make it 2.5D (3D on a 2D plane), but you could also make HD sprites if you are daring... but you can do it later, one step at a time.

- Add new worlds. Each classic 2D Mario was a brand new adventure. You must at the very least put back Giant World that was in Super Mario Bros 3 and in Galaxy, but not in NSMB (instead it got a lazy "moutain world"). Even better would be to create new worlds on par with it if you still set it in Mushroom Kingdom. but even more daring would be to completely make a new setting just like Super Mario Bros 2 (Subcon) and World (Dinosaur Land) and even Land (Sarasaland).
- I insist that there must be a strong adventure feeling. Super Mario Bros was a revolution. It had a huge world, was the first game with a blue sky, and popularised background music. It was an epic journey. There must be this feeling. In NSMB, it is bad that the levels feel disjointed. In original Mario, the world was continuous in that Mario went from one level to another by taking pipes (and not selecting on a screen). When maps were introduced in 3 and World, there were secrets in it. You didn't see everything in advance, but now you do in NSMB, even the secret warp zones, which is very bad! It doesn't make the player want to feel rewarded for exploring and using non-linear gameplay.
- A similar issue is content. Today, only gameplay is emphasized, and it is very good, but content, the world, the atmosphere, is just as important. Otherwise it feels too bland, like a bunch of "mechanics" strung together.

- In the good old Super Mario Bros 2, you could play with Peach. In NSMB, you can only play with 2D Toads!! and of course, in 3D Mario World, you can play with Peach... So finally put back fan-requested Peach where she belongs, in 2D Mario, and most importantly don't lazily shove in Miis, as it adds nothing but actually destroys the universe's coherence and the little immersion that remains.
-  Don't just add 2 power-ups and call it a day, when you give so much to 3D Mario. 3D World had fan-favorite Racoon Tail, but not NSMB U. Don't take random animals and blocks to make new power-ups. Each of them was very special. The Racoon Suit too (read Sean Malstrom's blog which analyzes it very well).

- No more kiddy tone. The enemies are dancing in tune with the cheery music, for god's sake! Original Mario was surreal, an Alice in Wonderland like experience, witty and whimsical, but not comical and goofy (like it is now, when Mario kicks a castle).. Mario was not Mickey Mouse smiling all the time with a high-pitched voice. He was a plumber thrown into this weird world and making his way through dinosaurs and tanks with his raised fist. When Bowser died, he fell in lava and it didn't shock. It made the final confrontation tense, the stakes being high. Now, it is completely wrong as Bowser is shown being fixed right after by his underlings to show he is fine, which is extreme censorship. It is lame, and even children prefer it the other way and have no problem with the big evil monster dinosaur dying. The penguin and cat suits are cool abilities, but just look play silly. The propeller suit as well.
- Mario was not a magical being either, but more akin to a normal dude (the power-ups he found on his way made him strong). So no more flying in space with fairy sparkles like in Galaxy. Don't put emphasis on Mario as a character. He is an avatar for the player. Don't or he will be considered lame. No story in general, but you seem to know that already, but it is just to make sure.

- To sum it up, make it just like you made classic Mario!
- Make at least two 2D Marios (it is very good that Switch will allow more games by being a hybrid). The first one should come at launch, and be accessible, not too hard, like the first Super Mario Bros and NSMB DS and Wii. The other game should be the expert Mario, like Super Mario Bros 3 and World, for the players familiar with it. If you only put the more complex Mario, there will be less people able to get into it, so put both in this order.
- You are allowed one or two 3D Mario, as long as their budget doesn't take away from 2D Mario.
- Mr. Miyamoto, you must be on board for this. Listen to young developer's ideas as well, those that wish for more 2D Mario and may understand its appeal on a gamer level. All people who bought NSMB in huge numbers assure you that your work, the most famous video game of all time and one of the very best, is not obsolete, its platformer gameplay works better in 2D than in 3D, so I sincerely hope you can enjoy making it. It is so much fun for us to play it.

Of course, you should do the same with Zelda and Metroid. Exactly the same. These are your most important classics series. They really need it.

Then, everyone will prosper like never before. If you take into account this, that the majority of gamers are asking, a golden age is upon us.
